policy_statement: Develop project planning and cost calculations to ensure maximum prices in project decisions do not impede cost-efficient energy-efficiency and emissions-reduction solutions (2018).
mechanism_description: It will develop facility project planning and cost calculations around energy efficiency and emissions reductions so that maximum price decisions do not hinder implementing cost-efficient efficiency and emissions-reduction solutions.
primary_causal_pathway: Same service (facility delivery/operation) → improved costing & investment choices → ↓ energy input → ↓ GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change: Public facility project planning, budgeting, and cost-calculation practices (e.g., moving from lowest-capex/max-price constraints to lifecycle cost and carbon-aware evaluation) so efficiency measures are not screened out. (2) Mediating process: More efficient building envelopes, systems, and equipment are selected/installed, reducing delivered energy set for the same level of service (heat, cooling, lighting, operations). (3) Outcome: Lower energy consumption during operation (and sometimes construction), leading to reduced fuel combustion and/or electricity generation emissions (and potentially lower peak demand).
dominant_pathway_test: Reforming project costing to prioritize lifecycle-efficient options most strongly reduces operational energy use, thereby cutting associated GHG emissions from energy supply.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Governance reforms change investment decisions so facilities deliver the same services with less energy, which then reduces energy-related emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While the end effect includes GHG mitigation, the mechanism’s defining lever is efficiency-driven reduction in energy input (an archetypal Resource Efficiency pathway) rather than a direct fuel switch or sequestration/hazard reduction.  
3) Edge cases applied: If the reform were used mainly to justify on-site renewables or fuel switching without reducing energy demand, the dominant pathway could shift toward Mitigation; however, the described mechanism explicitly centers on enabling cost-effective efficiency solutions.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy that reforms project costing/planning specifically to prevent price caps/maximum-price decisions from blocking cost-effective efficiency will primarily act by reducing energy use for the same service.  
5) Secondary subtype note: More specific subtypes (e.g., buildings vs. municipal infrastructure; procurement vs. budgeting; operational vs. embodied energy) require policy-level evidence and are deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Could blur into Mitigation if the costing reform is primarily used to select low-carbon energy supply (renewables/clean heat) rather than demand-side efficiency; could also include embodied-carbon effects if applied to materials procurement, but the stated mechanism is operational energy efficiency.
key_indicators: “project planning and cost calculations”; “developed from the perspective of energy efficiency and emissions reductions”; “maximum prices set in project decisions will not impede”; “implementation of cost-efficient solutions”
co_benefits: Lower operating costs/total cost of ownership; improved asset performance/comfort
instance_edge_case_notes: Could be interpreted as an internal performance/decision rule (a quasi-standard for budgeting), but the wording emphasizes development of planning/costing methodology rather than a quantified performance requirement; hence classified as planning-strategy.
